issues.title: Non-Timing-Safe Token Comparison in Gateway WebSocket Auth
issues.description: In src/gateway/auth.ts at line 174, the token-mode authentication check uses the JavaScript strict inequality operator (`connectAuth.token !== auth.token`) instead of the `safeEqual` function defined in the same file and correctly used for password comparison. This creates a timing side-channel that could allow an attacker to incrementally guess the gateway token character by character by measuring response latency differences.
issues.recommendation: Replace `connectAuth.token !== auth.token` with the existing `!safeEqual(connectAuth.token, auth.token)` call, which uses `timingSafeEqual` from `node:crypto` and is already used on line 188 for password comparison in the same function.
issues.evidence: The `safeEqual` helper (defined at line 34-37, wrapping `crypto.timingSafeEqual`) is used correctly for password mode on line 188 (`if (!safeEqual(password, auth.password))`), but the token mode check at line 174 uses plain `!==`. Both branches protect the same gateway endpoints. A co-located timing-safe implementation exists and is unused for the token path.

issues.title: Non-Timing-Safe Token Comparison in Hooks Endpoint Authentication
issues.description: In src/gateway/server-http.ts at line 85, the hooks endpoint authentication check uses JavaScript strict inequality (`token !== hooksConfig.token`) to validate the bearer/query-param/header-supplied hook token. This is a second timing side-channel vulnerability in addition to the gateway WebSocket token comparison. The hooks token controls access to triggering agent runs from external systems (GitHub Actions, Zapier, etc.).
issues.recommendation: Import and use the `safeEqual` helper (or `timingSafeEqual` from `node:crypto` directly) for the hooks token comparison, replacing `token !== hooksConfig.token` with a timing-safe equivalent.
issues.evidence: The comparison at line 85 is a plain string equality check (`token !== hooksConfig.token`). The `safeEqual` function exists in `src/gateway/auth.ts` and correctly uses `timingSafeEqual`. The hooks endpoint is HTTP-accessible and its token can be delivered via query parameter, making it more susceptible to network-level timing analysis than the WebSocket path.

issues.title: Hook Authentication Token Exposed in Server Logs via Query Parameter
issues.description: The hooks endpoint in src/gateway/hooks.ts (lines 69-70) accepts the authentication token via the `?token=` URL query parameter as a third delivery method alongside Authorization: Bearer and X-Clawdbot-Token headers. HTTP server access logs, reverse proxy logs, browser history, Referer headers, and external webhook-originating service logs routinely capture full URLs including query parameters. The hooks token (which grants full agent-run triggering capability) is therefore likely to be logged in plaintext whenever this method is used.
issues.code_snippet:
const queryToken = url.searchParams.get("token");
if (queryToken) return queryToken.trim();
issues.recommendation: Deprecate or remove query-parameter token delivery. Enforce token delivery only via the `Authorization: Bearer` header or `X-Clawdbot-Token` header. If query-parameter support must be retained, emit a prominent startup/per-request warning and document the log exposure risk.
issues.evidence: Lines 56-71 of `src/gateway/hooks.ts` show the `extractHookToken` function checking three sources in order: `Authorization` header, `X-Clawdbot-Token` header, then `url.searchParams.get('token')`. The function is called at line 84 of `src/gateway/server-http.ts` and the returned token is compared directly against `hooksConfig.token` at line 85. Any caller using the query parameter form embeds the secret in the URL, exposing it to any log consumer.

issues.title: Unsandboxed Bash Tool Executes Arbitrary Commands on Host OS
issues.description: The `exec` tool in src/agents/bash-tools.ts spawns shell commands using `spawn(shell, [...shellArgs, params.command])` on line 273, where `shell` is determined by `getShellConfig()` in src/agents/shell-utils.ts. On Unix, this defaults to `$SHELL` (the user's login shell, e.g., bash/zsh) with `-c` flag, enabling full shell interpretation. When the optional Docker sandbox (`defaults.sandbox`) is not configured — which is the typical deployment — commands run with full privileges of the gateway process's OS user. Any AI-generated tool call can execute arbitrary code on the host without confirmation.
issues.code_snippet:
spawn(shell, [...shellArgs, params.command], {
  cwd: workdir,
  env,
  detached: process.platform !== "win32",
  stdio: ["pipe", "pipe", "pipe"],
  windowsHide: true,
})
issues.recommendation: Enable Docker sandbox mode by default for all AI-triggered exec calls. When sandbox mode is disabled, require explicit config opt-in per-agent (already partially gated by `tools.elevated.enabled`). Add audit logging of every executed command string. Consider restricting network access in the sandbox container. Add a startup warning when the exec tool is enabled without a sandbox.
issues.evidence: Line 245 in `src/agents/bash-tools.ts` calls `getShellConfig()` which returns `{ shell: process.env.SHELL || 'sh', args: ['-c'] }` on Unix (src/agents/shell-utils.ts line 16-17). Line 273 passes the full AI-generated `params.command` string as a single shell argument, enabling pipes, redirections, subshells, and all shell features. The sandbox is only used when `defaults.sandbox` is set (line 255-279); otherwise the unsandboxed branch at line 273 runs directly on the host. The `elevated` gate only restricts the `elevated: true` parameter on tool calls, not baseline exec usage.

issues.title: Unsigned Plugin Code Loaded and Executed Without Sandbox or Integrity Check
issues.description: The plugin loader in src/plugins/loader.ts uses `jiti` (a TypeScript/ESM runtime loader) at line 281 to dynamically `require`/`import` plugin modules from file paths specified in the user config (`plugins.load.paths`). There is no code signature verification, no hash integrity check, no sandboxing (no `vm.runInNewContext` or Worker isolation), and no restriction on the Node.js APIs the plugin code can call. Any plugin code that passes the ID-based allow/deny check executes synchronously with full daemon OS privileges.
issues.recommendation: Implement hash-based integrity verification for plugin modules (record SHA-256 hash of plugin source on first load and warn/block on subsequent hash changes). Run plugin code in a restricted Worker thread or `vm.runInNewContext` sandbox with a limited API surface. Require explicit user confirmation when new plugin paths are added to the config.
issues.evidence: At line 253, `createJiti(import.meta.url, { interopDefault: true })` creates an unrestricted jiti loader. At line 281, `jiti(candidate.source)` loads and executes the module at `candidate.source` (a filesystem path from config). The `resolveEnableState` check at line 258 only performs a string ID comparison against allow/deny lists — it does not verify file content integrity. The loaded module's `register` function is called at line 362 with a full `api` object giving access to tool registration and gateway handler registration.

issues.title: Prompt Injection via Untrusted Messaging Platform Messages
issues.description: Inbound messages from WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack, and other providers are passed directly as the `message` parameter to `agentCommand` (the AI agent runner) after only allowlist/policy checks. There is no sanitization, content filtering, or instruction-isolation applied to the message text before it is sent to the AI model as the user turn. A malicious sender whose identity passes the `allowFrom` check — or any sender when `groupPolicy=open` or `dmPolicy=open` — can craft a message that instructs the AI to ignore its system prompt, exfiltrate data via the exec tool, or send messages to attacker-controlled destinations.
issues.recommendation: Apply layered defenses: (1) use AI provider tool-policy features to restrict available tools per sender trust level; (2) implement a confirmation step before destructive exec commands originating from messaging platforms; (3) restrict or disable the exec tool for group-chat-originated messages; (4) use a separate, lower-privilege system prompt context for messaging-platform requests vs. direct gateway requests.
issues.evidence: The `resolveCommandAuthorization` function in `src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts` checks `allowFromList` but only gates slash-commands (control commands), not the agent message itself. When `allowAll` is true (line 82: `allowFromList.length === 0 || allowFromList.some(entry => entry.trim() === '*')`), any sender can trigger agent execution. The message text flows directly through `runReplyAgent` to `agentCommand` with no content filtering between the inbound message and the AI model prompt.

issues.title: No Rate Limiting on Gateway and OpenAI-Compatible Endpoints Enables API Cost Exhaustion
issues.description: Neither the gateway WebSocket server nor the `/v1/chat/completions` HTTP endpoint in src/gateway/openai-http.ts implement per-connection, per-IP, or per-token rate limiting on inbound requests. Each accepted request triggers an AI inference call billed to the operator's API keys. An authenticated attacker (or any attacker when auth.mode is 'none' on loopback) can submit unlimited concurrent requests, exhausting API quotas and accumulating significant financial charges.
issues.recommendation: Implement per-IP and per-token rate limiting on all gateway endpoints. Add a configurable monthly/daily API spend cap that halts new inference requests when exceeded. Implement request queuing with a maximum concurrent request depth. Consider exposing a spend-alert webhook when usage exceeds configurable thresholds.
issues.evidence: The `handleOpenAiHttpRequest` function at line 167 of `src/gateway/openai-http.ts` performs auth check then immediately calls `agentCommand` (line 230) with no rate limiting, no concurrent request cap, and no spend tracking. The function is registered in `src/gateway/server-http.ts` at line 228 without any middleware. The gateway WebSocket handler in `server.ts` similarly has no connection count limit or per-connection message rate limiting — `MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES` (512KB, server-constants.ts) limits only individual frame size, not request frequency.

issues.title: Bot Tokens and API Keys Stored in Plaintext Config File
issues.description: All messaging platform credentials (Telegram bot token, Discord bot token, Slack bot token and signing secret), the gateway auth token/password, and the hooks token are stored in plaintext in ~/.clawdbot/clawdbot.json. A single file read via backup exposure, cloud sync (Dropbox/iCloud auto-sync of the home directory), or shell history leakage exposes all credentials simultaneously. The config file is the single point of compromise for the entire deployment.
issues.recommendation: Offer an encrypted credential store with OS keychain integration (macOS Keychain is already used for Anthropic OAuth tokens). Separate non-sensitive config from credentials. Add a startup check that warns if the config file has world-readable permissions (`chmod 600` enforcement). Document the credential file risk prominently in setup documentation.
issues.evidence: The config type definitions in `src/config/types.ts` include plaintext fields for `botToken`, `token`, `password`, and `signingSecret` across provider configs. The macOS Keychain integration noted in THREAT_MODEL.json is confirmed to be only for Anthropic OAuth/Codex OAuth tokens in `src/agents/cli-credentials.ts` (line 17: `CLAUDE_CLI_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE`). All other provider secrets (lines 273, 371 of `src/config/zod-schema.ts`) are read from and written to the plaintext JSON config file at `~/.clawdbot/clawdbot.json`.

issues.title: LAN Gateway Exposure: Startup Guard Present But Bypassed via Tailscale Path
issues.description: The gateway server.ts at line 542 does include a guard that throws an error when `bind != loopback` and `authMode == 'none'`. However, the Tailscale `allowTailscale` path in `auth.ts` (lines 149-164) creates a subtle bypass: when `auth.mode == 'none'` AND `auth.allowTailscale == true` AND the request is NOT a local-direct request, the code requires Tailscale headers. But if a request from a non-loopback LAN IP arrives (when bind=lan is somehow set with a token configured then token removed), `isLocalDirectRequest` returns false, `allowTailscale` check fires, and the Tailscale header check runs — but a LAN attacker who knows the header names can spoof them since there is no cryptographic verification of Tailscale headers beyond TCP origin being loopback.
issues.recommendation: The startup guard at server.ts:542 is a critical control and should be preserved. Additionally: (1) document clearly that the guard only applies at startup — if auth config is mutated at runtime via config reload, the guard may not re-run; (2) for the Tailscale path, verify that `isTailscaleProxyRequest` additionally checks that the source IP is exactly loopback (127.0.0.1/::1) to prevent header spoofing from LAN IPs.
issues.evidence: At server.ts line 542, `if (!isLoopbackHost(bindHost) && authMode === 'none')` throws an error — this effectively blocks LAN exposure with auth.mode=none at startup. However, `isTailscaleProxyRequest` at auth.ts line 97-103 checks `isLoopbackAddress(req.socket?.remoteAddress)` AND `hasTailscaleProxyHeaders(req)` — since LAN bind would require a non-loopback remoteAddress for LAN clients, the Tailscale bypass actually cannot be triggered from LAN clients (remoteAddress would be a LAN IP, not loopback). The startup guard is effective. This is flagged as medium because the guard only runs at startup and a config-reload vulnerability could re-introduce the risk.

issues.title: Sub-Agent Tasks Inherit Parent Tool Permissions Without Additional Authorization
issues.description: The `sessions_spawn` tool allows the main AI agent to spawn sub-agents with arbitrary task strings generated from the AI's own output. Sub-agents run with the same tool capabilities (exec, browser, message) as the parent agent. A prompt injection in the parent's conversation context can cause the parent to generate a malicious sub-agent task. The sub-agent executes this task without any human approval step or reduced-privilege context.
issues.code_snippet: // Sub-agents spawned via sessions_spawn inherit exec and browser tool access
issues.recommendation: Apply the same sender trust level to sub-agents as to the originating parent session. Require explicit config opt-in for sub-agent tool capabilities separate from parent capabilities. Add a sub-agent task audit log entry including the originating session key and full task string before execution begins. Consider adding a human-in-the-loop confirmation for sub-agent spawning when the task includes exec commands.
issues.evidence: The threat model documents that `sessions_spawn` is in `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts` and that sub-agents inherit parent tool permissions (except recursive spawning, blocked by `isSubagentSessionKey`). The gateway method list in `server.ts` line 246-303 includes `agent` and `agent.wait` as accessible methods. The `allowAgents` config restricts which agent IDs can be spawned but does not restrict the tool capabilities available to those sub-agents.
